title: Show Me Examples: Inferring Visual Concepts from Image Sets
gist: Apple ML Research's page bundles a new ECCV paper showing state-of-the-art vision-language models fail to infer shared visual concepts from example image sets (introducing the VICIS benchmark and a fix), alongside older snippets on Transformer 'expert unit' interpretability and synthetic-to-real image refinement.

insights.claim: Current state-of-the-art vision-language models can follow complex textual instructions but struggle to reason from purely visual context, failing to infer a shared concept from a small set of example images and apply it to a new query image.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Highlights a capability gap in leading VLMs (a widely-used category across the industry) that pure text-instruction scaling hasn't closed — relevant when assessing any vendor's few-shot visual reasoning claims.
insights.quote: Vision-language models (VLMs) can follow complex textual instructions, yet they struggle to reason from purely visual context.

insights.claim: When faced with ambiguous visual context, current VLMs often ignore the provided example images altogether or default to biased generations rather than correctly inferring the intended concept.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: A concrete failure mode (bias-defaulting under ambiguity) with product-safety and reliability implications for any deployment leaning on few-shot visual prompting.
insights.quote: often ignoring the visual context or defaulting to biased generations

insights.claim: Apple ML Research introduces Visual Concept Inference from Sets (VICIS), a new task/benchmark in which a model must generate images that preserve a concept shared across a small context set while staying consistent with a separate query image.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: A new benchmark for few-shot visual concept transfer could become a reference evaluation standard in an area that currently lacks one, giving Apple first-mover framing over how this capability gets measured industry-wide.
insights.quote: We introduce Visual Concept Inference from Sets (VICIS), a task that evaluates this capability.

insights.claim: Apple proposes a training framework and architecture that learns to infer visual concepts from image sets and extract concept-specific embeddings from query images, rather than relying on scaling text-instruction tuning.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Implies the fix for visual in-context reasoning is a distinct training paradigm (concept-embedding extraction), not just bigger instruction-tuned VLMs — a point to probe when evaluating competitors' roadmaps.

insights.claim: On synthetic data and large-scale ImageNet/WordNet data, Apple's proposed model produces more accurate and diverse outputs than baseline VLMs and generalizes to unseen concepts and modalities such as sketches.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Generalization to a non-photographic modality (sketches) suggests applicability beyond natural images, e.g. design/creative tools or sketch-to-image features.
insights.quote: generalizes to unseen concepts and modalities such as sketches

insights.claim: The VICIS paper (accepted at ECCV, published July 2026) is a collaboration between Apple ML Research staff (Josh Susskind, Miguel Angel Bautista Martin) and LMU Munich-affiliated researchers (Nick Stracke, Kolja Bauer, Björn Ommer).
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Shows Apple's continued pattern of co-publishing generative/vision AI research with European academic labs, a signal of its external talent and research pipeline strategy.

insights.claim: In separate 2021 research (BayLearn), Apple-affiliated researchers define 'expert units' as neurons in pretrained Transformer models that can classify a specific concept at a given average-precision threshold, and compiled a dataset of 1,641 concepts built on the OneSec dataset (Scarlini et al., 2019) to study this.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Indicates sustained Apple investment in mechanistic interpretability of Transformers — relevant context for evaluating Apple's broader AI-safety/explainability posture versus peers.
insights.quote: We define expert units to be neurons that are able to classify a concept with a given average precision, where a concept is represented by a binary set of sentences containing the concept (or not).

insights.claim: In earlier Apple research (July 2017), a method was developed to refine synthetic simulator-generated training images to look more realistic without added labeling cost, and models trained on these refined images showed significant accuracy improvements across various machine learning tasks versus training on raw synthetic images.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: This sim-to-real refinement lineage (predating and underlying later synthetic-data pipelines) shows how Apple scales training data cheaply without costly human annotation — an edge over labeling-heavy approaches.
insights.quote: training models on these refined images leads to significant improvements in accuracy on various machine learning tasks
